tagline: From Discovery to Delivery: Mastering the Enterprise Product Journey
audience: Product Managers, Engineering Leaders, Architects & Technical Decision-Makers
overview:
This program provides a comprehensive, end-to-end view of enterprise product delivery—from the first RFI to a stable production system. Drawing on proven frameworks from IIBA, SEI/CMMI, TOGAF, ITIL, and Google SRE practices, participants gain practical tools for every phase of the product lifecycle.

While the primary focus is software products, the training integrates hardware, firmware, and SOC (System-on-Chip) examples throughout—because modern enterprise products increasingly span the hardware-software boundary. Participants leave with actionable frameworks they can apply to their next product initiative immediately.
stats: 70% of enterprise IT projects experience significant scope creep; 45% exceed budget
Only 29% of projects are completed on time, on budget, and with required features
Organizations that invest in requirements engineering reduce rework by up to 50%
Companies with mature vendor evaluation processes save 15–30% on total cost of ownership

schedule.focus: The Discovery Phase: Requirements, RFI & Build-vs-Buy
Planning & Governance: From Contract to Kickoff

modules.title: The Discovery Phase
modules.subtitle: Module 1
modules.description: Map the full enterprise product lifecycle and understand where projects fail. Learn to distinguish needs from requirements, apply the MoSCoW prioritization method, and navigate the RFI/RFP/RFQ procurement hierarchy. Make informed Build-vs-Buy decisions using Total Cost of Ownership analysis.
modules.keyInsight: 70% of product failures trace back to poor requirements and unclear discovery—not bad engineering.
learningObjectives: Map the enterprise product lifecycle and identify common failure modes
Translate stakeholder needs into structured, traceable requirements
Navigate the RFI/RFP/RFQ procurement process effectively
Apply a structured Build-vs-Buy decision framework

topics.title: The Enterprise Product Lifecycle Map
topics.description: Overview of the full journey from need identification to production operations. Common failure modes: scope creep, misaligned expectations, vendor lock-in, and architecture drift. The lifecycle isn't linear—it's iterative with feedback loops at every phase. Hardware parallel: the V-Model lifecycle for systems with hardware-software integration.

topics.title: Requirements Engineering
topics.description: Functional vs. non-functional requirements. The MoSCoW prioritization method (Must have, Should have, Could have, Won't have). Requirements traceability matrix—linking every requirement to its business need and test case. Hardware example: embedded system requirements must specify operating temperature ranges, power consumption, physical dimensions, and EMC compliance alongside functional specs.

topics.title: The RFI/RFP/RFQ Framework
topics.description: RFI (Request for Information): market scanning and capability discovery. RFP (Request for Proposal): detailed solution proposals with technical approach and pricing. RFQ (Request for Quote): price-focused procurement for well-defined specifications. When to use each and how to structure them. SOC example: for SOC-as-a-Service procurement, the RFI explores vendor SIEM capabilities and threat intelligence sources before the RFP specifies detection rules, response SLAs, and integration requirements.

topics.title: Build vs. Buy Decision Framework
topics.description: Total Cost of Ownership analysis: license/build costs, integration, customization, maintenance, training, opportunity cost. Strategic vs. commodity capabilities—build for differentiation, buy for standardization. The hidden costs of both paths. Hardware considerations: COTS (Commercial Off-The-Shelf) vs. custom hardware design—when each makes sense for sensors, controllers, and edge devices.

modules.title: Vendor Evaluation & Architecture Design
modules.subtitle: Module 2
modules.description: Evaluate vendors systematically using weighted scoring matrices and Proof-of-Concept protocols. Design enterprise architectures that balance current needs with future scalability. Define integration strategies and API contracts. Build security and compliance into the architecture from day one.
modules.keyInsight: Architecture decisions made in the first 10% of a project determine 90% of its long-term cost and flexibility.
learningObjectives: Evaluate vendors using structured scoring and POC methodology
Select appropriate architecture patterns for enterprise contexts
Design integration strategies and API contracts
Embed security and compliance into architecture decisions

topics.title: Vendor Evaluation Methodology
topics.description: Weighted scoring matrices with criteria: technical fit, scalability, vendor viability, support quality, integration capability, and total cost. Proof of Concept (POC) vs. Proof of Value (POV)—validating both technical feasibility and business impact. Reference checks, financial due diligence, and escape clause planning. SOC example: evaluating SIEM vendors on detection coverage, false positive rates, log ingestion capacity, and SOAR integration.

topics.title: Enterprise Architecture Patterns
topics.description: Monolith vs. microservices vs. modular monolith—when each is appropriate. Event-driven architecture for real-time processing. Data architecture patterns: data mesh for domain ownership, data lake for analytics, data warehouse for reporting. The architecture decision record (ADR) practice. Hardware parallel: System-on-Chip (SoC) architecture decisions—IP core selection, bus architecture (AMBA, AXI), memory hierarchy, and the build-vs-license tradeoff for silicon IP.

topics.title: Integration Strategy & API Design
topics.description: API-first design principles for enterprise systems. Integration patterns: point-to-point (simple but brittle), ESB/integration platform (centralized), event bus (decoupled, scalable). Data format standards: REST for CRUD, GraphQL for flexible queries, gRPC for high-performance internal communication. Hardware protocols: MQTT and CoAP for IoT device communication; CAN bus, SPI, I2C for embedded system integration.

topics.title: Security Architecture & Compliance by Design
topics.description: Threat modeling using STRIDE (Spoofing, Tampering, Repudiation, Information Disclosure, Denial of Service, Elevation of Privilege). Security architecture patterns: zero trust, defense in depth, least privilege. Compliance frameworks: SOC 2 Type II (trust service criteria), ISO 27001 (information security management), GDPR (data protection). Building security into architecture rather than bolting it on after the fact.

modules.title: Planning & Governance
modules.subtitle: Module 3
modules.description: Navigate contract negotiations with confidence—defining scope, milestones, SLAs, and IP ownership. Select the right delivery methodology for your context. Identify, assess, and mitigate risks systematically. Define clear team structures with RACI matrices for cross-functional delivery.
modules.keyInsight: The best contract is one that both parties hope they never need to reference—because the governance structure makes collaboration natural.
learningObjectives: Negotiate contracts with clear scope, milestones, and SLA definitions
Select delivery methodologies appropriate to project context
Apply structured risk identification and mitigation frameworks
Define clear roles and responsibilities using the RACI model

topics.title: Contract Negotiation & SLAs
topics.description: Key contract elements: scope definition, milestone-based payments, acceptance criteria, intellectual property ownership, change management procedures, and exit clauses. SLA design: availability targets (99.9% vs. 99.99% and what the difference means), response time tiers, escalation paths, and penalty/incentive structures. Hardware: warranty terms, mean-time-to-repair (MTTR) commitments, spare parts agreements, and end-of-life notification periods.

topics.title: Delivery Methodology Selection
topics.description: Agile (Scrum/Kanban) for iterative discovery with fast feedback. SAFe for large-scale coordination across multiple teams. Waterfall for regulatory-heavy or hardware-dependent deliverables. Hybrid approaches that combine the best of each. The methodology should serve the product, not the other way around. Hardware: V-Model for systems requiring hardware-software co-design; stage-gate process for physical product development with manufacturing considerations.

topics.title: Risk Management & Mitigation
topics.description: Risk identification using pre-mortem analysis and risk taxonomy. Impact × Probability matrix for prioritization. Four mitigation strategies: Avoid (eliminate the risk source), Transfer (insurance, contractual allocation), Mitigate (reduce probability or impact), Accept (conscious decision with contingency). SOC example: risk assessment for security tool migration—what happens if the SIEM goes down during cutover? How do you maintain threat detection capability during the transition window?

topics.title: Team Structure & RACI
topics.description: RACI matrix: Responsible (does the work), Accountable (owns the outcome), Consulted (provides input before), Informed (notified after). Cross-functional team design: product, engineering, QA, security, operations. Vendor-client team integration models: embedded, liaison, independent. Scaling principles: the two-pizza team at scale, Conway's Law (your architecture will mirror your team structure), and inverse Conway maneuver.

modules.title: Build & Quality
modules.subtitle: Module 4
modules.description: Design effective CI/CD pipelines with clear environment strategies. Implement quality gates at every lifecycle phase using the test pyramid. Integrate security validation (SAST, DAST, SCA) into the development workflow. Plan for performance from the start with systematic load testing and capacity planning.
modules.keyInsight: Quality is not a phase—it's a discipline that runs through every stage of the lifecycle.
learningObjectives: Design CI/CD pipelines and environment strategies
Implement quality gates using the test pyramid
Integrate security scanning into the development workflow
Plan and execute performance engineering from the start

topics.title: Development Lifecycle Management
topics.description: Sprint/iteration planning and backlog grooming. CI/CD pipeline design: build, test, scan, deploy as automated stages. Environment strategy: development, integration, staging, pre-production, production—each with clear purpose and data policies. Feature flagging for progressive delivery and safe experimentation. Hardware: firmware development workflows with hardware-in-the-loop (HIL) testing, FPGA prototyping cycles, and the challenge of long hardware lead times alongside agile software sprints.

topics.title: Quality Gates & Testing Strategy
topics.description: The test pyramid: many unit tests (fast, cheap), fewer integration tests (medium), fewer end-to-end tests (slow, expensive). Quality gates at each lifecycle phase: code review, static analysis, test coverage thresholds, performance benchmarks. Acceptance criteria and Definition of Done as team contracts. Hardware: environmental testing (thermal cycling, vibration, humidity), reliability testing (MTBF/MTTF calculations), compliance testing (FCC, CE marking, UL certification).

topics.title: Security Validation & Compliance
topics.description: SAST (Static Application Security Testing): finding vulnerabilities in source code. DAST (Dynamic Application Security Testing): testing running applications. SCA (Software Composition Analysis): identifying vulnerable dependencies. Penetration testing cadence and scope. SOC 2 audit preparation: evidence collection, control documentation, gap remediation. Hardware: hardware security modules (HSM), secure boot chains, firmware signing, and tamper detection mechanisms.

topics.title: Performance Engineering
topics.description: Performance testing types: load (expected traffic), stress (beyond limits), endurance (sustained load), spike (sudden bursts). Capacity planning: sizing infrastructure based on projected growth and peak scenarios. Benchmarking and baseline establishment for regression detection. Observability-driven development: instrument before you ship. Hardware: thermal throttling analysis, power consumption profiling under load, signal integrity testing at speed, and EMI compliance under operating conditions.

modules.title: Go-Live & Production Operations
modules.subtitle: Module 5
modules.description: Execute deployments safely using blue-green, canary, and rolling strategies. Conduct production readiness reviews with clear go/no-go criteria. Build observability and incident management practices. Establish continuous improvement loops that drive product evolution.
modules.keyInsight: Launch day isn't the finish line—it's the starting line. The best products are built by teams that operate what they build.
learningObjectives: Select and execute appropriate deployment strategies
Conduct production readiness reviews
Build observability and incident management practices
Establish continuous improvement and product evolution loops

topics.title: Deployment Strategies & Production Readiness
topics.description: Blue-green deployment: two identical environments, instant switchback. Canary releases: gradual rollout to a subset of users. Rolling deployments: incremental instance-by-instance updates. Production Readiness Review (PRR): runbooks, monitoring, alerting, rollback procedures, and on-call rotation. Go/no-go criteria: all quality gates passed, performance benchmarks met, security scan clean, documentation complete. Hardware: phased hardware rollouts by site, pilot site selection criteria, field upgrade procedures, and firmware rollback mechanisms.

topics.title: Observability & Incident Management
topics.description: The three pillars of observability: metrics (what's happening), logs (why it happened), traces (where it happened). SLO/SLI/SLA hierarchy: SLIs measure behavior, SLOs set targets, SLAs create contracts. Alerting strategy: alert on symptoms not causes, avoid alert fatigue. Incident management: detection, triage, mitigation, resolution, post-mortem. Blameless post-mortems that focus on system improvement, not individual blame.

topics.title: Continuous Improvement & Product Evolution
topics.description: Feedback loops: user analytics, support ticket analysis, NPS scores, customer advisory boards. Technical debt management: tracking, prioritizing, and allocating capacity for debt reduction. Product roadmap governance: balancing new features, technical health, and operational improvements. End-of-life planning: data migration, customer communication, and graceful deprecation. The product is never 'done'—it evolves or it dies.

frameworks.name: Requirements Traceability Matrix
frameworks.application: Linking requirements to business needs and test cases

frameworks.name: RACI Matrix
frameworks.application: Clarifying roles and responsibilities in cross-functional teams

frameworks.name: Test Pyramid
frameworks.application: Balancing test coverage with execution speed

frameworks.name: CI/CD Pipeline Design
frameworks.application: Automating build, test, and deployment workflows

frameworks.name: Blameless Post-Mortem
frameworks.application: Learning from incidents to improve systems

designHighlights: Real-world case studies from enterprise product deliveries
Hands-on RFI/RFP evaluation exercises using realistic scenarios
Architecture design workshops with trade-off analysis
Personal product lifecycle action plan with prioritized next steps
facilitatorBio: Cihat Tombak brings over 15 years of experience in software development leadership at global multinational companies. Having led teams through dozens of enterprise product deliveries—from greenfield builds to complex vendor integrations and legacy modernizations—he provides practical, battle-tested frameworks for navigating the full product lifecycle.

logistics.format: Interactive workshop with tables of 6-10 participants
logistics.materials: Participant workbook, RFI/RFP templates, vendor scorecard template, architecture decision record template, risk register template
